version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: memcontrol: propagate NMI slab stats to memcg vmstats

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the memcontrol code where NMI slab stats are not propagated to memcg vmstats, leading to inconsistent statistics. The fix involves modifying the flush_nmi_stats function to update both per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: memcontrol: propagate NMI slab stats to memcg vmstats
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses an inconsistency in the propagation of NMI slab statistics to memcg-level vmstats. The current implementation, flush_nmi_stats(), only drains per-node NMI slab atomics into per-node lruvec_stats but fails to update the corresponding memcg-level vmstats. This patch fixes this issue by propagating the NMI slab stats to both per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/8] mm: percpu: charge obj_exts allocation with __GFP_ACCOUNT
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the per-cpu memory allocation system to charge the metadata and payload allocations separately, allowing for more accurate accounting of memory usage. The patch does this by passing the __GFP_ACCOUNT flag to the obj_exts allocation and removing its accounting in pcpu_memcg_pre_alloc_hook(). This is a preparatory step for upcoming per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. The changes affect two files: mm/percpu-internal.h and mm/percpu.c, with 11 insertions and 20 deletions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/8] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits memory group (memcg) charging and kernel memory (kmem) accounting into two separate steps to improve per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. The first step, obj_cgroup_precharge(), reserves pages for memcg limit enforcement without updating kmem stats. The second step, obj_cgroup_account_kmem(), accounts kmem after the allocation succeeds and places any remaining sub-page remainder into the object's stock. This decoupling allows for temporary overcharging of memcg but ensures that kmem accounting is accurate in the long run.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/8] mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the MEMCG_KMEM feature and instead introduces per-node obj_cgroup objects to track memory usage. It does not convert existing kmem users to proper per-memcg-per-node accounting, which is handled in subsequent patches. The changes include updates to memcontrol.h, mmzone.h, and memcontrol.c files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/8] mm: memcontrol: per-node kmem accounting for page charges
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the __memcg_kmem_charge_page function to use per-node object groups for correct NUMA attribution of kernel memory. The change involves introducing a new variable nid_objcg, which is used to get the object group associated with the node where the page resides. This ensures that kernel memory charges are attributed correctly based on the node where the page is allocated.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/8] mm: slab: per-node kmem accounting for slab
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the slab hook to use per-node object cgroups for accurate NUMA attribution of kernel memory usage (NR_KMEM). It achieves this by introducing a new nid_objcg variable, which retrieves the per-node object cgroup based on the node ID of the slab. The patch modifies several functions within the memcontrol.c file to utilize the nid_objcg instead of the global objcg. This change ensures that kernel memory usage is attributed correctly to each NUMA node, providing a more accurate picture of system resource utilization.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting using local credit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node kmem accounting using a local credit system to handle precharged pages in the percpu memory allocation code. The previous approach used obj_stock, which is no longer suitable due to decoupling of memcg charging and kmem accounting. The new approach ensures that enough charged pages are available for allocation by maintaining a node-specific credit that gets refilled into the stock when necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the zswap and zsmalloc modules to use per-node object cgroup for kernel memory accounting, ensuring that compressed page charges are attributed to the correct NUMA node. However, it does not correctly handle entries that span multiple pages, which will be addressed in a separate series by Joshua Hahn. The patch introduces a new function `zs_handle_to_nid` to determine the NUMA node associated with a given zsmalloc handle and updates the `zswap_store_page` function to use per-node object cgroup for charging. This change is necessary to accurately account for memory usage on systems with multiple NUMA nodes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-per-node kernel memory accounting to associate memcg and physical memory usage. It builds on recent changes to obj_cgroup, which ties object groups to their NUMA node. The patches split kmem charging and accounting for percpu allocations, and convert other kmem users such as slab and zswap to use the new per-node accounting. This allows for more accurate tracking of kernel memory usage at a per-node level.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concerns about the performance impact of the patch, specifically suggesting that expensive operations would benefit from benchmarking to demonstrate kernel allocation overhead.

Reviewer Joshua Hahn expressed concerns about the performance implications of splitting obj_cgroup to be per-node, which would invalidate the obj_stock cache and lead to increased atomic operations. He also questioned the need for iterating over num_possible_cpus() x pages_per_alloc.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Alex,

Thank you for your work!

Overall I think the direction makes sense to me. Pre-overcharging makes sense to
me as an approach, we would much rather overaccount than underaccount and
later have to breach limits.

I do have some concerns on performance, though. Namely, I think there are
some expensive operations that I think would benefit from some performane
benchmarking with this patch added (maybe some simple microbenchmarks that
demonstrates kernel allocation overhead could be useful).

---

to do with iterating over num_possible_cpus() x pages_per_alloc, which
doesn't seem trivial to me.

Another concern that I see is the stock credit system. Maybe we could be
bypassing the stock check leading to more time spent doing the atomic
operations.

obj_stock caches a single obj_cgroup, which means that if we split the objcg
to be per-node (in patch 6), then the obj_stock basically gets invalidated
every operation since we iterate over more objcgs (even though we are in
the same logical objcg). Maybe I'm missing something?

I haven't taken a deep look at the implementation details but just wanted to
raise some high level items that I noticed. Of course, all of these concerns
are just theoretical, if you can show that the performance delta is not
noticable then all of my concerns don't matter.

I also want to talk more about the local credit system but let's first see
what the numbers are first.

Thanks again, Alex. And I really like patch 2 because it is a solution to
a problem that I ran into in my percpu tracking series that I couldn't think
of before! Thank you for solving my problem too : -)

Have a great day!
Joshua

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds four test cases to the Btrfs file system to detect and prevent various race conditions that can occur when deleting a qgroup (quota group) while writing quotas. The tests cover scenarios where an extent's owner reference outlives its subvolume, leading to potential underflows or transaction aborts. The patch aims to ensure correct quota accounting by rejecting qgroup deletion requests in these situations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch addresses an issue where defrag_mode in the page allocator fails to handle non-reclaimable allocations, leading to SLUB allocation failures under network-heavy workloads. It does this by clearing ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT and retrying the allocation when direct reclaim is not possible.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Raised a question about the potential impact of the change on concurrent cpuset or zonelist alterations, and also pointed out a possible conflict with a later comment in the code.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 18 May 2026 16:37:36 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> When defrag_mode is enabled, ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT is enforced to prevent
> migratetype fallbacks and keep pageblocks clean. The allocator relies on
> reclaim and compaction to free pages of the correct type before allowing
> fallback as a last resort.
> 
> However, non-reclaimable allocations such as GFP_ATOMIC cannot invoke
> direct reclaim or compaction. With defrag_mode=1, these allocations hit
> the !can_direct_reclaim bailout in __alloc_pages_slowpath() with
> ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT still set, and fail without ever attempting a fallback.
> 
> This causes a large number of SLUB allocation failures for
> skbuff_head_cache under network-heavy workloads, despite free memory
> being available in other migratetype freelists.
> 
> Clear ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT and retry before giving up on allocations that
> cannot reclaim, following the same pattern used after reclaim/compaction
> exhaustion later in the slowpath.

Thanks.  Sashiko asked a couple of things:

	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260518163736.173910-1-d@ilvokhin.com

I'm not sure what to make of the first one - we aren't holding any locks
in there which prevent concurrent cpuset or zonelist alterations
anyway (?).

But your change might violate the later comment `No "goto retry;" can be
placed above this check * unless it can execute just once'?

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch converts the nvdimm_bus guard from a DEFINE_GUARD() to a class-based implementation using DEFINE_CLASS() and DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(). This change is necessary because the current implementation accepts NULL values, which will cause a compile error when the constructor is made __nonnull(). The patch makes no functional changes.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to remove dead NULL checks from unconditional guard destructors in the Linux kernel. The patches prepare guards that handle NULL, add __nonnull() attributes for compile-time enforcement, and finally remove the unnecessary checks.

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda
review_comments.summary: Identified a discrepancy in the documentation links for __nonnull() attributes and suggested correcting them. Also, pointed out that the attribute does not prevent NULL pointers from being passed.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 18, 2026 at 5:22 PM Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
>
> Add __nonnull() to unconditional guard constructors so the compiler
> verifies at each call site that NULL is never passed:

> This provides automated, compiler-enforced verification that no
> unconditional guard constructor receives NULL.

I wouldn't say "verify", since the compiler does a best-effort here
with the information it has statically.

In other words, the attribute does not prevent NULL pointers to be passed.

> + *   gcc: https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Function-Attributes.html#index-nonnull-function-attribute

Hmm... It appears GCC has changed the docs in commit 6e3c137f5dbb
("doc: Merge function, variable, type, and statement attribute
sections [PR88472]"), dropping the per-kind attribute pages.

So the right link would need to be now:

  https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Attributes.html#index-nonnull

I will need to send a patch to fix the other links.

> + * clang: https://clang.llvm.org/docs/AttributeReference.html#nonnull

I think this link goes to `_Nonnull` -- the GNU one is instead:

  https://clang.llvm.org/docs/AttributeReference.html#id10

(I don't love the numeric IDs, though, since they break, so I think it
is fine either way -- the `_Nonnull` is fairly close to the one we
want and I hope that one doesn't break)

> + */
> +#define __nonnull(x...)                        __attribute__((__nonnull__(x)))

This is indeed available for a long time, and we already use it
elsewhere in the kernel tree (which would be nice to clean up
separately).

If you don't mind, please place it before `__nonstring__` (the file is
meant to be sorted by the actual attribute name -- there are a few
instances where this is not the case anymore, which I will eventually
clean up)

Thanks!

Cheers,
Miguel

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/21] fuse: introduce FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO mode
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO mode, which enforces a one-to-one mapping between fuse inodes and backing file inode numbers. This mode is only supported on 64-bit architectures where the inode number is a u64 type. It allows the kernel to instantiate fuse inodes by passthrough lookup and readdirplus, and notifies the server about these inodes using their backing file inode numbers as unique identifiers. The patch modifies several files in the FUSE codebase to support this new mode, including fs/fuse/file.c, fs/fuse/fuse_i.h, fs/fuse/inode.c, and include/uapi/linux/fuse.h.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/21] fuse: prepare for passthrough of inode operations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the fuse filesystem to pass through inode operations by adding a new field 'ops_mask' to the FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN ioctl input argument. The ops_mask declares which inode operations will be passed through to the backing file once it's attached to the fuse inode on lookup. Currently, only regular files are allowed for read/write/splice/mmap passthrough, but this patch relaxes that restriction by allowing non-regular backing files when setting operations other than FUSE_READ/FUSE_WRITE in ops_mask. Additionally, a filesystem with FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO is required to set up passthrough of any inode operations for simplification of inode attribute caching.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author suggests decoupling passthrough operation codes from fuse opcode definitions, proposing a new approach where passthrough operations are defined as capabilities rather than specific opcodes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Do you think we should couple the passthrough op code to the fuse
opcode so closely, instead of defining the passthrough ops separately,
eg doing something like this?:

#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_READ         (1 << 0)
#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_WRITE       (1 << 1)

In the (far) future when some more advanced passthrough features get
added (eg full subtree passthrough), it seems like we'd want to add a
passthrough op for that, but that wouldn't map to a fuse op. I think
there are also some fuse ops we might skip defining as passthrough ops
but are implicitly passed through (eg FUSE_STATX which is covered by
FUSE_GETATTR), so it seems more intuitive to define the passthrough
ops as describing capabilities rather than implying that it describes
what specific opcodes get passed through?

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Amir Goldstein requested that the _OP_ suffix not be dropped, citing the need to distinguish it from other constants and potentially using a larger data type for ops_mask.

Reviewer questioned the duplication of information between the flags field and the ops_mask field in the fuse_backing_map structure, suggesting it is unnecessary to include flags in ops_mask.

Reviewer Amir Goldstein noted that the ops_mask field in FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN ioctl could also be used for other operations like FUSE_CREATE and FUSE_CREAT, and suggested leaving it as a u64 to avoid potential issues with reserved space.

The reviewer suggested replacing the hardcoded arithmetic in UAPI with a macro-based approach, providing examples of how to use macros for ease of use and suggesting that the current implementation is limiting.
review_comments.raw_body:
Don't drop the _OP_ please
Need it to distinguish from FUSE_PASSTHROUGH{,_INO}
and we need to think if we want to format this as 32bit and extend later
or start with a u64 ops_mask format from the start.

---

I don't know. fuse_backing_map has flags, why would we want to put that
in ops_mask.

---

This could also become the case with FUSE_CREATE{,_HANDLE}
or {FUSE,FUSEX}_CREAT.

TBH, the fact that some ops will never be in the mask and
that some ops have a canonical bit in itself does not justify creating
a different mapping. Do we also want to squeeze the mask into 32bit
and leave reserved space? my intuition is to leave it u64.

---

hmm. I guess you have a point that it is a bit limiting to commit
to this arithmetic in UAPI.

My thinking behind this was ease of use with these helpers:

       if (!fuse_passthrough_op(file_inode(in), FUSE_READ))

but those could just as well be macros:

static inline bool fuse_inode_passthrough_op(struct inode *inode,
                                            u64 opbit)...

#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP(inode, opname)    \
             fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_ ## opname)

#define FUSE_BACKING_MAP_OP(map, opname) \
       ((map)->ops_mask & FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP ## opname))

...
       if (!FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP(file_inode(in), READ))

wdyt?

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agrees with the macro idea and suggests using u32 for ops_mask, but is open to reviewer's preference.
sentiment_signals: happy to go with what you think
review_comments.raw_body:
Nice, the macro idea is neat!

I feel like it gives more flexibility to define it separately and I
think with that, u32 would be more than enough bits as well. But I'm
happy to go with what you think would be better here.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Amir Goldstein expressed uncertainty about changing the type of ops_mask from u64 to something else, suggesting that it's not a pressing concern since it's in an ioctl and could be reconsidered if backing_map responses are moved inline in lookup/readdirplus response.
review_comments.raw_body:
I am not sure. Let's keep it u64 for now.
Since the struct is in an ioctl, it does not really matter much.
If we ever consider moving backing_map responses inline
in lookup/readdirplus response (maybe over io uring) we could reconsider.

In case you missed the exciting conversations about this API
original fuse passthough patches from Android had the backing fd
in the open response but it was deemed unsafe writing fds to
/dev/fuse, so ioctl was chosen instead [1].

With io_uring I don't think the security concern holds, so I see no reason
why backing_map would not be possible in-place for readdirplus
responses if we wanted to do that, but we can also ignore all this for now.

Thanks,
Amir.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/21] fuse: prepare for readdir passthrough on directories
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module to support readdir passthrough on directories. It allows directory inodes to have their inode iomode state applicable, and modifies the fuse_sync_release() helper function to handle directories. The changes enable cached mode, direct uncached readdir mode, and readdir passthrough mode for directories, but do not require waiting for parallel dio like regular files. This patch is a step towards implementing readdir passthrough on directories, which will improve performance by reducing the number of system calls required.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/21] fuse: implement passthrough for readdir
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements passthrough for readdir operations in the FUSE file system. It allows a directory to be opened with both cached readdir and readdir passthrough enabled, but not at the same time as regular files. The opt-in for passthrough of READDIR operation means that there is no READDIRPLUS call to the server. This patch also includes changes to ensure that directories opened without both FOPEN_CACHE_DIR and FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH flags are marked as FOPEN_DIRECT_IO, which does not affect io mode.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/21] fuse: prepare for long lived reference on backing file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) code to support a long-lived reference to the backing file of an inode, which will be used for passthrough operations and detached when the inode is evicted. The changes include modifications to the fuse_i.h header file, the inode.c file, and the iomode.c file to accommodate this new feature. This patch is part of a larger series of patches that aim to improve the FUSE code's handling of backing files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/21] fuse: implement passthrough for getattr/statx
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements passthrough functionality for the getattr and statx operations in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module. The patch allows the FUSE driver to forward these requests directly to the underlying filesystem, rather than processing them itself. This is done by calling vfs_getattr() on the backing inode to retrieve the requested attributes, which are then used to update the fuse inode attributes. The patch also introduces a new function, fuse_kstat_to_attr(), that converts a kstat structure into a fuse attribute structure. The passthrough functionality is enabled for getattr and statx operations by setting the FUSE_GETATTR flag in the fuse inode's flags. This approach simplifies the implementation of these operations and reduces the overhead associated with attribute caching.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein

review_comments.raw_body:
I think you've corrected enough of my flop in this patch to justify
Signed-of-by, but as you wish :)

Thanks,
Amir.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 07/21] fuse: prepare to setup backing inode passthrough on lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a helper function to associate a backing inode for inode passthrough operations in the FUSE file system. The helper, fuse_backing_id_get, retrieves a fuse backing object by its ID and increments its reference count. This is done to prepare for setting up backing inode passthrough on lookup responses. The minimal requirement for the backing inode is to support GETATTR passthrough operations. Unlike open-time setup, this mapping is created for the entire lifetime of the inode and cannot be changed later.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 08/21] fuse: handle zero ops_mask in FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses various issues in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation, including handling zero ops_mask in FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN. The patch specifically introduces a default value for ops_mask to maintain backwards compatibility with servers that pre-date this field.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 09/21] fuse: handle partial io passthrough for read/write, splice, and mmap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows servers to pass through only reads or writes instead of both, avoiding cache coherency issues by separating the backing file's page cache from the fuse inode's page cache. It also rejects mmap operations when passthrough is partial or not set for read/writes since mmap requires both read and write access. The patch includes self-contained fallback logic for handling direct io when passthrough is not enabled.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 10/21] fuse: prepare to cache statx attributes from entry replies
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the fuse kernel module to cache statx attributes from entry replies by adding a new argument to the fuse_iget function, which will store the statx information in the inode's cache. The change is made in preparation for a future patch that will add a new struct fuse_entry2_out outarg containing filled-out statx information returned by the server on LOOKUP/MKDIR/MKNOD requests. Currently, all callers pass NULL as the argument, but this will be updated to use the new statx information once it is available.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 11/21] fuse: clean up fuse_dentry_revalidate()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch cleans up the fuse_dentry_revalidate function by removing gotos with direct returns and flattening a nested if/else structure, resulting in improved code readability without any functional changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 12/21] fuse: add struct fuse_entry2_out and helpers for extended entry replies
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new extended entry reply structure called fuse_entry2_out, which carries additional information such as backing_id and statx attributes. It also introduces helpers to process this new structure for passthrough support in lookup, revalidate, and create operations. The existing fuse_statx_to_attr function is moved earlier in the file to avoid forward declarations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 13/21] fuse: add passthrough lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds passthrough lookup support to the FUSE file system. When a backing ID is set for a fuse inode, it associates the inode with the corresponding backing inode for passthrough operations. If no backing ID is set, it caches statx attributes from the reply. The changes are made in the fs/fuse/dir.c file and involve modifications to the fuse_process_entry2 function and the fuse_dentry_revalidate function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 14/21] fuse: add passthrough support for entry creation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for passthrough operations in the FUSE file system, specifically for entry creation operations such as mknod, mkdir, symlink, and link. When FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO is enabled, it uses the new extended fuse_entry2_out reply to associate a newly created inode with a backing file for passthrough operations. If no backing id is returned, statx attributes are cached instead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 15/21] fuse: add passthrough support for create+open
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds passthrough support for create and open operations in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver. When FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO is enabled, it uses the extended fuse_entry2_out reply to associate a newly created inode with a backing file for passthrough operations before instantiating the dentry. This allows for more efficient handling of create and open requests by reusing existing resources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 16/21] fuse: allow backing_id=0 in open to inherit inode's backing file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the FUSE server to pass in backing_id=0 in the open response, which enables inheriting the inode's backing file. This is done by checking if passthrough_ino is set and using the existing backing file on the inode. The change affects the fuse_passthrough_open function.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating that it looks good to them.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 17/21] backing-inode: add backing_inode_copyattr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a generic function, backing_inode_copyattr(), to the Linux kernel's fs/backing-inode.c file. The function copies inode attributes from a backing inode to a new inode, taking into account idmappings if present. This allows other filesystems that use backing inodes (such as fuse passthrough) to reuse this logic. The patch also moves the relevant code from overlayfs/util.c to the new generic function.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 18/21] backing-inode: add backing_inode_setattr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a generic function called backing_inode_setattr() to handle setattr operations on backing inodes. The function is responsible for updating the attributes of the underlying inode, while also handling locking and write access. This allows other filesystems that use backing inodes (such as fuse passthrough) to reuse this logic. The patch moves the existing ovl_setattr() function's logic into this new generic function, making it easier to maintain and extend.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 19/21] fuse: add passthrough setattr
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a passthrough setattr feature to the FUSE file system, which allows setting attributes directly on the backing inode instead of sending a FUSE_SETATTR request to the server. This is done by checking for passthrough setattr before stripping suid/sgid permissions in the handle_killpriv/handle_killpriv_v2 functions. The patch introduces new functions and macros to support this feature, including fuse_passthrough_setattr and FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_SETATTR.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged a suggestion to gate FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO on FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2, but disagreed that it was necessary due to calling fuse_passthrough_setattr() in the wrong place. Instead, she proposed having the backing filesystem handle suid/sgid stripping atomically through its own notify_change() path.
review_comments.raw_body:
In the v1 discussion [1], there was a suggestion about gating
FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO on FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2 to avoid the
non-atomic killpriv path, but I think we were only encountering the
non-atomic killpriv path because I was calling
fuse_passthrough_setattr() in the wrong place. I think we can avoid
this altogether by just having the backing filesystem handle all of
the suid/sgid stripping atomically through its own notify_change()
path, which will let us skip all the fuse killpriv handling logic in
this function.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/CAOQ4uxipQJt5zskb_THxueGi_MXpFdywFiGpd_nmWeY_sMHwzQ@mail.gmail.com/

review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Amir Goldstein questioned the necessity of a separate patch for handling FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO and FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2, suggesting that if the server requires privilge removal, it will likely apply attribute changes to the backing inode, making FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2 the natural choice. He also pointed out that a similar logic applies to ATOMIC_O_TRUNC.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think it still matters, but to the only-passthrough-GETATTR case
and I am still in favor of making this change.
My point was why have the discussion whether getattr
for the purpose of killpriv should be passthrough or not (next patch)?
Why not drop the next patch instead because it will become moot.

If FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO requires FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2
it means that FUSE kernel is never trying to play games removing privs
itself, not with passthrough and not without passthrough.

Quoting your comment on next patch
"I think there might be some use cases
though where having only getattr passed through and not setattr is
useful (eg server wants fast stat() but needs to intercept attribute
changes for access control or policy enforcement)."

Valid use case, but in this valid use case, said server may reject/allow
attribute changes, but eventually if it allows them it will likely apply
them to the very file which was set as backing inode, so essentially
FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2 is the natural choice for a
FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO server, even when it wants to introspect
setattr.

I think the same is true for ATOMIC_O_TRUNC.
Either backing fs deals with O_TRUNC or server deals with O_TRUNC.

But this is my opinion - I am open to hearing other opinions.

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that handling KILLPRIV_V2 stripping in the server is not burdensome, agreed to require FUSE_KILLPRIV_V2 for passthrough inode operations in v3.
review_comments.raw_body:
I was thinking from the user perspective, it'd be nicer to not couple
things unless it was absolutely necessary, especially since
passthrough is a per-inode/per-file attribute whereas KILLPRIV_V2 is a
global server configuration (eg server might want passthrough on just
a few files without taking on killpriv responsibility for the whole
filesystem).

But I think everything you wrote makes sense and I don't think
handling the stripping stuff is too burdensome to ask of the server.

For v3, I'll make the change to require FUSE_KILLPRIV_V2 for
FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 20/21] fuse: use passthrough getattr in setattr suid/sgid handling
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series modifies the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module to handle suid/sgid permissions more efficiently when performing setattr operations. Specifically, it introduces a passthrough getattr mechanism that allows the server to calculate the new mode for setattr based on its own getattr request.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 21/21] docs: fuse: document extended passthrough (FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically about extended passthrough mode (FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO). This new feature allows for inode operations and directory operations to be passed through from the user space to the kernel, with a one-to-one mapping between fuse inode numbers and backing file inode numbers. The documentation explains how to enable this feature, including steps for negotiating the capability during FUSE_INIT, registering backing files, and handling requests in the daemon.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that the documentation incorrectly states that files opened with FOPEN_DIRECT_IO deny entry into both caching and passthrough IOMODE, when in fact they only deny entry into caching.

Reviewer noted that the requirement for FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH when opening files in caching mode without doing IO passthrough could be perplexing to users, and suggested either documenting this limitation or relaxing the restriction.
review_comments.raw_body:
More accurately, no open files in caching or passthough.
files open with FOPEN_DIRECT_IO may exist and they
do not deny entering either caching or passthrough iomode.

---

I had not considered this.
Setting OP_GETATTR/SETATTR in LOOKUP without any OP_RW bits
and then requiring that the server open files with FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH
with the backing_id sounds strange -
I understand why it was done this way, but for users this could be perplexing.

The thing is that if the server is only doing GETATTR passthrough there is not
really a reason to deny open file in caching mode (I think?).

The limitation that requires FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH is encoded in
fuse_file_cached_io_open()
    fuse_is_io_cache_wait()
        !fuse_inode_backing()

We have two options.

Either we decide that server passing through GETATTR and opening
files in caching mode is not interesting to support (for now) and then just
maybe we need to better document the reason that server needs to
use FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH despite not doing io passthrough.

Or we relax the limitation w.r.t cached io mode, something like this
(completely untested most likely broken based or earlier review
suggestion regarding fuse_passthrough_op):

Thanks,
Amir.

 }

@@ -54,7 +55,7 @@ int fuse_file_cached_io_open(struct inode *inode,
struct fuse_file *ff)

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series extends the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) passthrough feature to support inode operations, including getattr, setattr, and readdir. The existing passthrough mode is modified to introduce a stricter mode called FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO, which requires one-to-one inode number mapping for attaching a backing file to a fuse inode. This allows for more efficient handling of inode operations and improves test results in certain scenarios.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the one-to-one inode number mapping in FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO mode, explaining that it requires 64-bit inode support and will limit the possibility of mapping multiple fuse inodes to the same backing file unless they are hardlinks. The author did not indicate any plans for a fix or revision.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the interaction between FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH and FOPEN_CACHE_DIR flags in directory opens, explaining that both flags are required for readdir passthrough and that a directory opened without both flags is marked as FOPEN_DIRECT_IO. The author noted that this behavior is similar to regular files.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about maintaining backwards compatibility for servers that pre-date the ops_mask field. They agreed to default the ops_mask to FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS in such cases, while requiring an explicit ops_mask for FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO servers.
review_comments.raw_body:
Servers that pre-date the ops_mask field will pass a zero ops_mask in
FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN. Default this to FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS to
maintain backwards compatibility.

For FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO servers, an ops_mask must be set.
FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO is a new feature with no backwards compatibliity
requirements.

Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
---
 fs/fuse/backing.c | 20 +++++++++++++++++---
 1 file changed, 17 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/fuse/backing.c b/fs/fuse/backing.c
index 2234ab47406a..b499860e1185 100644
--- a/fs/fuse/backing.c
+++ b/fs/fuse/backing.c
@@ -119,9 +119,23 @@ int fuse_backing_open(struct fuse_conn *fc, struct fuse_backing_map *map)
 	if (map->flags || map->ops_mask & ~FUSE_BACKING_MAP_VALID_OPS)
 		goto out;
 
-	/* For now passthrough inode operations requires FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO */
-	if (!fc->passthrough_ino && map->ops_mask & FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INODE_OPS)
-		goto out;
+	if (!fc->passthrough_ino) {
+		/*
+		 * For now passthrough inode operations requires
+		 * FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO
+		 */
+		if (map->ops_mask & FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INODE_OPS)
+			goto out;
+		/*
+		 * To maintain backwards compatibility with servers that
+		 * pre-date the ops_mask field, a zero ops_mask defaults
+		 * to passing through both reads and writes
+		 */
+		if (!map->ops_mask)
+			map->ops_mask |= FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS;
+	} else if (!map->ops_mask) {
+		return -EINVAL;
+	}
 
 	file = fget_raw(map->fd);
 	res = -EBADF;
-- 
2.52.0

---

Servers can now pass through only reads or writes instead of being
required to pass through both. When the read or write operation is not
passed through, fall back to direct io for handling it. This avoids
cache coherency issues from mixing the backing file's page cache with
the fuse inode's page cache.

Reject mmap when passthrough is partial or not set for read/writes since
mmap requires both read and write access to the backing page cache.

The FOPEN_DIRECT_IO check in the read/write fallback is not strictly
needed as the caller already handles it, but including it here makes
the fallback logic self-contained.

Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
---
 fs/fuse/file.c        |  7 ++++---
 fs/fuse/fuse_i.h      |  3 +++
 fs/fuse/passthrough.c | 18 ++++++++++++++++++
 3 files changed, 25 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

index 1173811b2ea7..5b70d8272700 100644

+++ b/fs/fuse/file.c
@@ -1781,7 +1781,7 @@ static ssize_t __fuse_direct_read(struct fuse_io_priv *io,
 
 static ssize_t fuse_direct_IO(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *iter);
 
-static ssize_t fuse_direct_read_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *to)
+ssize_t fuse_direct_read_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *to)
 {
 	ssize_t res;
 
@@ -1796,7 +1796,7 @@ static ssize_t fuse_direct_read_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *to)
 	return res;
 }
 
-static ssize_t fuse_direct_write_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from)
+ssize_t fuse_direct_write_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from)
 {
 	struct inode *inode = file_inode(iocb->ki_filp);
 	ssize_t res;
@@ -1884,7 +1884,8 @@ static ssize_t fuse_splice_write(struct pipe_inode_info *pipe, struct file *out,
 	struct fuse_file *ff = out->private_data;
 
 	/* FOPEN_DIRECT_IO overrides FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH */
-	if (fuse_file_passthrough(ff) && !(ff->open_flags & FOPEN_DIRECT_IO))
+	if (fuse_passthrough_op(file_inode(out), FUSE_WRITE) &&
+	    !(ff->open_flags & FOPEN_DIRECT_IO))
 		return fuse_passthrough_splice_write(pipe, out, ppos, len, flags);
 	else
 		return iter_file_splice_write(pipe, out, ppos, len, flags);
diff --git a/fs/fuse/fuse_i.h b/fs/fuse/fuse_i.h
index 9e5142a94a09..a1034533ce60 100644
--- a/fs/fuse/fuse_i.h
+++ b/fs/fuse/fuse_i.h
@@ -1261,6 +1261,9 @@ struct fuse_file *fuse_file_open(struct fuse_mount *fm, u64 nodeid,
 void fuse_file_release(struct inode *inode, struct fuse_file *ff,
 		       unsigned int open_flags, fl_owner_t id, bool isdir);
 
+ssize_t fuse_direct_read_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *to);
+ssize_t fuse_direct_write_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from);
+
 /* passthrough.c */
 
 /* READ/WRITE are implied by FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH, but defined for completeness */
diff --git a/fs/fuse/passthrough.c b/fs/fuse/passthrough.c
index f2025772c9c1..d62a1c751157 100644
--- a/fs/fuse/passthrough.c
+++ b/fs/fuse/passthrough.c
@@ -44,6 +44,10 @@ ssize_t fuse_passthrough_read_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *iter)
 	if (!count)
 		return 0;
 
+	if ((ff->open_flags & FOPEN_DIRECT_IO) ||
+	    !fuse_passthrough_op(file_inode(file), FUSE_READ))
+		return fuse_direct_read_iter(iocb, iter);
+
 	ret = backing_file_read_iter(backing_file, iter, iocb, iocb->ki_flags,
 				     &ctx);
 
@@ -70,6 +74,10 @@ ssize_t fuse_passthrough_write_iter(struct kiocb *iocb,
 	if (!count)

> +       if (fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_SETATTR))
> +               return fuse_passthrough_setattr(entry, attr);

In the v1 discussion [1], there was a suggestion about gating
FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO on FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2 to avoid the
non-atomic killpriv path, but I think we were only encountering the
non-atomic killpriv path because I was calling
fuse_passthrough_setattr() in the wrong place. I think we can avoid
this altogether by just having the backing filesystem handle all of
the suid/sgid stripping atomically through its own notify_change()
path, which will let us skip all the fuse killpriv handling logic in
this function.

Thanks,
Joanne

I left this part untouched from v1 but there was a discussion [1]
about whether to enforce that setattr must be passed through if
getattr is passed through [1]. I think there might be some use cases
though where having only getattr passed through and not setattr is
useful (eg server wants fast stat() but needs to intercept attribute
changes for access control or policy enforcement). I'm not sure if you
still feel it's better to keep it simple with enforcing that

so I'm happy to add that in.

On Fri, May 15, 2026 at 5:52 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> From: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>
>
> So far, fuse passthrough was implemented for read/write/splice/mmap
> operations for regular files opened with FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH.
>
> A backing file is attached to a fuse inode, but only for as long as
> there are FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH files opened on this inode.
>
> We would like to attach a backing file to fuse inode also without an
> open file to allow passthrough of some inode operations.
>
> Add field ops_mask to the input argument of FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN
> ioctl to declare the operations that would passthrough to the backing
> file once it has been attached to the fuse inode on lookup.
>
> Setting the FUSE_READ/FUSE_WRITE operations in the ops_mask is not
> required because those operations are implied by FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH.
>
> When setting operations other than FUSE_READ/FUSE_WRITE in ops_mask,
> non-regular backing files are allowed, so we need to verify when
> attaching a backing file to a fuse inode, that their file types match.
>
> For simplification of inode attribute caching, for now, require a
> filesystem with FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO (one-to-one mapping from fuse inode
> to backing inode) for setting up passthrough of any inode operations.
> We may consider relaxing this requirement for some inode operations
> in the future.
>
> Reviewed-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
> Signed-off-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>
> ---
>  fs/fuse/backing.c         | 13 ++++++++++---
>  fs/fuse/fuse_i.h          | 30 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
>  fs/fuse/iomode.c          | 16 ++++++++++++++++
>  include/uapi/linux/fuse.h |  9 ++++++++-
>  4 files changed, 64 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/include/uapi/linux/fuse.h b/include/uapi/linux/fuse.h
> index 4be9ccc5b3ff..0f1e1c1ec367 100644
> --- a/include/uapi/linux/fuse.h
> +++ b/include/uapi/linux/fuse.h
> @@ -243,6 +243,7 @@
>
> +#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP(op)        (1ULL << ((op) - 1))
> +
> +/* op bits for fuse_backing_map ops_mask */
> +#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_READ       FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP(FUSE_READ)
> +#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_WRITE      FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP(FUSE_WRITE)

Do you think we should couple the passthrough op code to the fuse
opcode so closely, instead of defining the passthrough ops separately,
eg doing something like this?:

#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_READ         (1 << 0)
#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_WRITE       (1 << 1)

In the (far) future when some more advanced passthrough features get
added (eg full subtree passthrough), it seems like we'd want to add a
passthrough op for that, but that wouldn't map to a fuse op. I think
there are also some fuse ops we might skip defining as passthrough ops
but are implicitly passed through (eg FUSE_STATX which is covered by
FUSE_GETATTR), so it seems more intuitive to define the passthrough
ops as describing capabilities rather than implying that it describes
what specific opcodes get passed through?

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that server passing through GETATTR and opening files in caching mode is not currently supported, but proposed two options: either document the reason for requiring FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH or relax the limitation by allowing cached io mode.

On Sat, May 16, 2026 at 1:59 PM Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Sat, May 16, 2026 at 8:23 PM Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > On Sat, May 16, 2026 at 2:53 AM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> > >
> > > Add section about extended passthrough (FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO) mode.
> > >
> > > Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
> > > ---
> > >  .../filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst     | 126 ++++++++++++++++++
> > >  1 file changed, 126 insertions(+)
> > >
> > > diff --git a/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst b/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst
> > > index 2b0e7c2da54a..751d27c6fc5c 100644
> > > --- a/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst
> > > +++ b/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst
> > > @@ -25,6 +25,11 @@ operations.
> > >
> > > +Extended Passthrough (FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO)
> > > +============================================
> > > +
> > > +``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` is a stricter variant of ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH`` in
> > > +which the backing file inode number must match the fuse inode number, enforcing
> > > +a one-to-one mapping. The kernel offers this flag during ``FUSE_INIT`` if
> > > +``CONFIG_FUSE_PASSTHROUGH`` is enabled and the architecture has 64-bit
> > > +``ino_t``. The daemon accepts by returning it back in the init reply.
> >
> > IIUC, ino_t is now always 64-bit.

When Jeff's changes for u64 inode->i_ino land in the fuse tree, I'll
get rid of this and update the 1st patch accordingly.

> >
> > > +
> > > +IO Mode State Machine
> > > +---------------------
> > > +
> > > +The ``iocachectr`` field in ``struct fuse_inode`` prevents conflicting access
> > > +modes on the same inode (page-cache I/O and passthrough I/O cannot coexist)::
> > > +
> > > +    iocachectr > 0    Cached mode
> > > +    iocachectr == 0   Idle. No files open, no passthrough
> >
> > More accurately, no open files in caching or passthough.
> > files open with FOPEN_DIRECT_IO may exist and they
> > do not deny entering either caching or passthrough iomode.

Good point, I'll update this to be more precise.

> >
> > > +    iocachectr < 0    Uncached/passthrough mode
> > > +
> > > +Each open file in passthrough mode holds one reference (``iocachectr--``).
> > > +The inode-level passthrough setup holds one additional long-lived reference
> > > +if the backing has inode ops (getattr/setattr). This long-lived reference is
> > > +released on inode eviction.
> > > +
> > > +Cached mode and passthrough mode are mutually exclusive. Attempting either
> > > +while the other is active returns ``-ETXTBSY``.
> > > +
> > > +For directories, the same mechanism arbitrates between cached readdir
> > > +(``FOPEN_CACHE_DIR``) and passthrough readdir. A directory opened without
> > > +``FOPEN_CACHE_DIR`` and without ``FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH`` is treated as direct I/O
> > > +and does not affect io mode.
> > > +
> > > +Things to note
> > > +--------------
> > > +
> > > +- ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` requires 64-bit ``ino_t``.
> >
> > Not relevant I think.
> >
> > > +- Readdirplus does not set up inode passthrough. Inodes created via readdirplus
> > > +  use normal FUSE operations until a fresh lookup occurs.
> > > +- An inode's backing association is set once and cannot be changed.
> > > +- Passthrough and cached I/O cannot coexist on the same inode.
> > > +- If any inode operations are passed through, this means all opened files need
> > > +  to set the ``FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH`` flag in the open response, even if reads
> > > +  and writes are not passed through. If reads and writes are not passed
> > > +  through, they will go directly to the daemon.
> >
> > I had not considered this.
> > Setting OP_GETATTR/SETATTR in LOOKUP without any OP_RW bits
> > and then requiring that the server open files with FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH
> > with the backing_id sounds strange -
> > I understand why it was done this way, but for users this could be perplexing.
> >
> > The thing is that if the server is only doing GETATTR passthrough there is not
> > really a reason to deny open file in caching mode (I think?).
> >
> > The limitation that requires FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH is encoded in
> > fuse_file_cached_io_open()
> >     fuse_is_io_cache_wait()
> >         !fuse_inode_backing()
> >
> > We have two options.
> >
> > Either we decide that server passing through GETATTR and opening
> > files in caching mode is not interesting to support (for now) and then just
> > maybe we need to better document the reason that server needs to
> > use FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH despite not doing io passthrough.
> >
> > Or we relax the limitation w.r.t cached io mode, something like this
> > (completely untested most likely broken based or earlier review
> > suggestion regarding fuse_passthrough_op):
> >
> > Thanks,
> > Amir.
> >
> > diff --git a/fs/fuse/iomode.c b/fs/fuse/iomode.c
> > index c5f3e2201c6ab..7c33e7a974f6c 100644
> > --- a/fs/fuse/iomode.c
> > +++ b/fs/fuse/iomode.c
> > @@ -20,7 +20,8 @@ static inline bool fuse_is_io_cache_wait(struct inode *inode)
> >         struct fuse_inode *fi = get_fuse_inode(inode);
> >
> >         return S_ISREG(inode->i_mode) &&
> > -               READ_ONCE(fi->iocachectr) < 0 && !fuse_inode_backing(fi);
> > +               READ_ONCE(fi->iocachectr) < 0 &&
> > +               !fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS);
> >  }
>
> Obviously, this was naive and not close to enough, because
> fuse_inode_set_passthrough() still takes the negative iocachectr
> refcount.
>
> Please ignore this. Supporting caching iomode and GETATTR
> passthrough is left for a future exercise, if anyone is ever interested.

Sounds good, I will continue to leave this unsupported for now.

Thanks for reviewing these patches.
>
> Thanks,
> Amir.

> > non-regular backing files are allowed, so we need to verify when

> > filesystem with FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO (one-to-one mapping from fuse inode

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the documentation does not clearly explain how to handle passthrough operations when the inode number is changed, and requested clarification on this point.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, May 16, 2026 at 2:53 AM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Add section about extended passthrough (FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO) mode.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
> ---
>  .../filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst     | 126 ++++++++++++++++++
>  1 file changed, 126 insertions(+)
>
> diff --git a/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst b/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst
> index 2b0e7c2da54a..751d27c6fc5c 100644
> --- a/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst
> @@ -25,6 +25,11 @@ operations.
>  Currently, passthrough is supported for operations like ``read(2)``/``write(2)``
>  (via ``read_iter``/``write_iter``), ``splice(2)``, and ``mmap(2)``.
>
> +With the extended ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` mode, passthrough is also supported
> +for inode operations (getattr, setattr) and directory operations (readdir).
> +In this mode, a backing file is attached to a fuse inode for its entire
> +lifetime.
> +
>  Enabling Passthrough
>  ====================
>
> @@ -46,6 +51,127 @@ To use FUSE passthrough:
>       the ``backing_id`` to release the kernel's reference to the backing file
>       when it's no longer needed for passthrough setups.
>
> +Extended Passthrough (FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO)
> +============================================
> +
> +``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` is a stricter variant of ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH`` in
> +which the backing file inode number must match the fuse inode number, enforcing
> +a one-to-one mapping. The kernel offers this flag during ``FUSE_INIT`` if
> +``CONFIG_FUSE_PASSTHROUGH`` is enabled and the architecture has 64-bit
> +``ino_t``. The daemon accepts by returning it back in the init reply.

IIUC, ino_t is now always 64-bit.

> +
> +Enabling Extended Passthrough
> +-----------------------------
> +
> +To use extended passthrough:
> +
> +  1. Follow steps 1-2 from `Enabling Passthrough`_ above. The daemon must
> +     also negotiate the ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` capability during
> +     ``FUSE_INIT``.
> +  2. When registering a backing file via ``FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN``, set
> +     the ``ops_mask`` field in ``struct fuse_backing_map`` to declare which
> +     operations should be passed through. At minimum,
> +     ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_GETATTR`` must be set for any inode-level
> +     passthrough.
> +  3. When handling a ``LOOKUP``, ``CREATE``, ``MKNOD``, ``MKDIR``,
> +     ``SYMLINK``, or ``LINK`` request, the daemon responds with a
> +     ``fuse_entry2_out`` (instead of ``fuse_entry_out``). To enable
> +     passthrough on the inode, set ``backing_id`` to the id returned by
> +     ``FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN``. Set ``backing_id`` to 0 for inodes
> +     that should not use passthrough. The ``nodeid`` in the response must
> +     be the backing file's inode number (``i_ino``). If they don't match,
> +     the kernel rejects the passthrough setup with ``-EIO``.
> +  4. When handling an ``OPEN`` request for a FUSE file, the daemon
> +     replies with the ``FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH`` flag set in
> +     ``fuse_open_out::open_flags`` and provides the corresponding ``backing_id``
> +     in ``fuse_open_out::backing_id`` or leaves ``fuse_open_out::backing_id``
> +     blank. If the daemon would like to opt out of passthrough when the inode
> +     is already in passthrough mode, it may additionally set
> +     ``FOPEN_DIRECT_IO``, which will forward read/write operations directly to
> +     the daemon.
> +  5. The FUSE daemon should eventually call ``FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_CLOSE`` with
> +     the ``backing_id`` to release the kernel's reference to the backing file
> +     when it's no longer needed for passthrough setups.
> +
> +Passthrough Operations Mask
> +---------------------------
> +
> +When registering a backing file via ``FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN``, the daemon
> +sets ``ops_mask`` in ``struct fuse_backing_map`` to declare which operations
> +should be passed through::
> +
> +    FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_READ
> +    FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_WRITE
> +    FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_READDIR
> +    FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_GETATTR
> +    FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_SETATTR
> +
> +Operations fall into two categories, which can be combined:
> +
> +**Inode operations** (getattr, setattr): Activated on lookup when the daemon
> +returns a ``backing_id`` in the ``fuse_entry2_out`` response. The backing
> +file reference persists for the lifetime of the fuse inode. Getattr is the
> +minimum required inode operation.
> +
> +**File operations** (read, write, readdir): Require the file to be opened with
> +``FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH`` in the daemon's open response. Read and write can be set
> +independently for partial passthrough. If only one direction is set, the other
> +falls back to direct IO and mmap is disabled.
> +
> +Extended Entry Reply
> +--------------------
> +
> +When ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` is negotiated, the kernel uses
> +``fuse_entry2_out`` instead of ``fuse_entry_out`` for entry responses.
> +This struct carries a ``backing_id`` and ``fuse_statx`` attributes instead
> +of ``fuse_attr``.
> +
> +When ``backing_id > 0``, the kernel associates the inode with the backing file
> +for passthrough inode operations. Statx attributes are not cached because
> +passthrough getattr fetches them directly from the backing inode.
> +
> +When ``backing_id == 0`` (no passthrough), the statx attributes from the reply
> +are cached normally.
> +
> +A negative ``backing_id`` is treated as an error. The kernel sends
> +``FUSE_FORGET`` for the returned nodeid and fails the operation.
> +
> +IO Mode State Machine
> +---------------------
> +
> +The ``iocachectr`` field in ``struct fuse_inode`` prevents conflicting access
> +modes on the same inode (page-cache I/O and passthrough I/O cannot coexist)::
> +
> +    iocachectr > 0    Cached mode
> +    iocachectr == 0   Idle. No files open, no passthrough

More accurately, no open files in caching or passthough.
files open with FOPEN_DIRECT_IO may exist and they
do not deny entering either caching or passthrough iomode.

> +    iocachectr < 0    Uncached/passthrough mode
> +
> +Each open file in passthrough mode holds one reference (``iocachectr--``).
> +The inode-level passthrough setup holds one additional long-lived reference
> +if the backing has inode ops (getattr/setattr). This long-lived reference is
> +released on inode eviction.
> +
> +Cached mode and passthrough mode are mutually exclusive. Attempting either
> +while the other is active returns ``-ETXTBSY``.
> +
> +For directories, the same mechanism arbitrates between cached readdir
> +(``FOPEN_CACHE_DIR``) and passthrough readdir. A directory opened without
> +``FOPEN_CACHE_DIR`` and without ``FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH`` is treated as direct I/O
> +and does not affect io mode.
> +
> +Things to note
> +--------------
> +
> +- ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` requires 64-bit ``ino_t``.

Not relevant I think.

> +- Readdirplus does not set up inode passthrough. Inodes created via readdirplus
> +  use normal FUSE operations until a fresh lookup occurs.
> +- An inode's backing association is set once and cannot be changed.
> +- Passthrough and cached I/O cannot coexist on the same inode.
> +- If any inode operations are passed through, this means all opened files need
> +  to set the ``FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH`` flag in the open response, even if reads
> +  and writes are not passed through. If reads and writes are not passed
> +  through, they will go directly to the daemon.

I had not considered this.
Setting OP_GETATTR/SETATTR in LOOKUP without any OP_RW bits
and then requiring that the server open files with FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH
with the backing_id sounds strange -
I understand why it was done this way, but for users this could be perplexing.

The thing is that if the server is only doing GETATTR passthrough there is not
really a reason to deny open file in caching mode (I think?).

The limitation that requires FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH is encoded in
fuse_file_cached_io_open()
    fuse_is_io_cache_wait()
        !fuse_inode_backing()

We have two options.

Either we decide that server passing through GETATTR and opening
files in caching mode is not interesting to support (for now) and then just
maybe we need to better document the reason that server needs to
use FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH despite not doing io passthrough.

Or we relax the limitation w.r.t cached io mode, something like this
(completely untested most likely broken based or earlier review
suggestion regarding fuse_passthrough_op):

Thanks,
Amir.

diff --git a/fs/fuse/iomode.c b/fs/fuse/iomode.c
index c5f3e2201c6ab..7c33e7a974f6c 100644
--- a/fs/fuse/iomode.c
+++ b/fs/fuse/iomode.c
@@ -20,7 +20,8 @@ static inline bool fuse_is_io_cache_wait(struct inode *inode)
        struct fuse_inode *fi = get_fuse_inode(inode);

        return S_ISREG(inode->i_mode) &&
-               READ_ONCE(fi->iocachectr) < 0 && !fuse_inode_backing(fi);
+               READ_ONCE(fi->iocachectr) < 0 &&
+               !fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS);
 }

 /*
@@ -54,7 +55,7 @@ int fuse_file_cached_io_open(struct inode *inode,
struct fuse_file *ff)
         * Check if inode entered passthrough io mode while waiting for parallel
         * dio write completion.
         */
-       if (fuse_inode_backing(fi)) {
+       if (fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS)) {
                clear_bit(FUSE_I_CACHE_IO_MODE, &fi->state);
                spin_unlock(&fi->lock);
                return -ETXTBSY;
@@ -285,7 +286,8 @@ int fuse_file_io_open(struct file *file, struct
inode *inode)
         * which is already open for passthrough.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that ino_t is now always 64-bit, which contradicts the documentation stating that FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO requires a one-to-one mapping between fuse inode number and backing file inode number.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, May 16, 2026 at 8:23 PM Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Sat, May 16, 2026 at 2:53 AM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > Add section about extended passthrough (FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO) mode.
> >
> > Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
> > ---
> >  .../filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst     | 126 ++++++++++++++++++
> >  1 file changed, 126 insertions(+)
> >
> > diff --git a/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst b/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst
> > index 2b0e7c2da54a..751d27c6fc5c 100644
> > --- a/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst
> > +++ b/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-passthrough.rst
> > @@ -25,6 +25,11 @@ operations.
> >  Currently, passthrough is supported for operations like ``read(2)``/``write(2)``
> >  (via ``read_iter``/``write_iter``), ``splice(2)``, and ``mmap(2)``.
> >
> > +With the extended ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` mode, passthrough is also supported
> > +for inode operations (getattr, setattr) and directory operations (readdir).
> > +In this mode, a backing file is attached to a fuse inode for its entire
> > +lifetime.
> > +
> >  Enabling Passthrough
> >  ====================
> >
> > @@ -46,6 +51,127 @@ To use FUSE passthrough:
> >       the ``backing_id`` to release the kernel's reference to the backing file
> >       when it's no longer needed for passthrough setups.
> >
> > +Extended Passthrough (FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO)
> > +============================================
> > +
> > +``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` is a stricter variant of ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH`` in
> > +which the backing file inode number must match the fuse inode number, enforcing
> > +a one-to-one mapping. The kernel offers this flag during ``FUSE_INIT`` if
> > +``CONFIG_FUSE_PASSTHROUGH`` is enabled and the architecture has 64-bit
> > +``ino_t``. The daemon accepts by returning it back in the init reply.
>
> IIUC, ino_t is now always 64-bit.
>
> > +
> > +Enabling Extended Passthrough
> > +-----------------------------
> > +
> > +To use extended passthrough:
> > +
> > +  1. Follow steps 1-2 from `Enabling Passthrough`_ above. The daemon must
> > +     also negotiate the ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` capability during
> > +     ``FUSE_INIT``.
> > +  2. When registering a backing file via ``FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN``, set
> > +     the ``ops_mask`` field in ``struct fuse_backing_map`` to declare which
> > +     operations should be passed through. At minimum,
> > +     ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_GETATTR`` must be set for any inode-level
> > +     passthrough.
> > +  3. When handling a ``LOOKUP``, ``CREATE``, ``MKNOD``, ``MKDIR``,
> > +     ``SYMLINK``, or ``LINK`` request, the daemon responds with a
> > +     ``fuse_entry2_out`` (instead of ``fuse_entry_out``). To enable
> > +     passthrough on the inode, set ``backing_id`` to the id returned by
> > +     ``FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN``. Set ``backing_id`` to 0 for inodes
> > +     that should not use passthrough. The ``nodeid`` in the response must
> > +     be the backing file's inode number (``i_ino``). If they don't match,
> > +     the kernel rejects the passthrough setup with ``-EIO``.
> > +  4. When handling an ``OPEN`` request for a FUSE file, the daemon
> > +     replies with the ``FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH`` flag set in
> > +     ``fuse_open_out::open_flags`` and provides the corresponding ``backing_id``
> > +     in ``fuse_open_out::backing_id`` or leaves ``fuse_open_out::backing_id``
> > +     blank. If the daemon would like to opt out of passthrough when the inode
> > +     is already in passthrough mode, it may additionally set
> > +     ``FOPEN_DIRECT_IO``, which will forward read/write operations directly to
> > +     the daemon.
> > +  5. The FUSE daemon should eventually call ``FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_CLOSE`` with
> > +     the ``backing_id`` to release the kernel's reference to the backing file
> > +     when it's no longer needed for passthrough setups.
> > +
> > +Passthrough Operations Mask
> > +---------------------------
> > +
> > +When registering a backing file via ``FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN``, the daemon
> > +sets ``ops_mask`` in ``struct fuse_backing_map`` to declare which operations
> > +should be passed through::
> > +
> > +    FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_READ
> > +    FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_WRITE
> > +    FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_READDIR
> > +    FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_GETATTR
> > +    FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_SETATTR
> > +
> > +Operations fall into two categories, which can be combined:
> > +
> > +**Inode operations** (getattr, setattr): Activated on lookup when the daemon
> > +returns a ``backing_id`` in the ``fuse_entry2_out`` response. The backing
> > +file reference persists for the lifetime of the fuse inode. Getattr is the
> > +minimum required inode operation.
> > +
> > +**File operations** (read, write, readdir): Require the file to be opened with
> > +``FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH`` in the daemon's open response. Read and write can be set
> > +independently for partial passthrough. If only one direction is set, the other
> > +falls back to direct IO and mmap is disabled.
> > +
> > +Extended Entry Reply
> > +--------------------
> > +
> > +When ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` is negotiated, the kernel uses
> > +``fuse_entry2_out`` instead of ``fuse_entry_out`` for entry responses.
> > +This struct carries a ``backing_id`` and ``fuse_statx`` attributes instead
> > +of ``fuse_attr``.
> > +
> > +When ``backing_id > 0``, the kernel associates the inode with the backing file
> > +for passthrough inode operations. Statx attributes are not cached because
> > +passthrough getattr fetches them directly from the backing inode.
> > +
> > +When ``backing_id == 0`` (no passthrough), the statx attributes from the reply
> > +are cached normally.
> > +
> > +A negative ``backing_id`` is treated as an error. The kernel sends
> > +``FUSE_FORGET`` for the returned nodeid and fails the operation.
> > +
> > +IO Mode State Machine
> > +---------------------
> > +
> > +The ``iocachectr`` field in ``struct fuse_inode`` prevents conflicting access
> > +modes on the same inode (page-cache I/O and passthrough I/O cannot coexist)::
> > +
> > +    iocachectr > 0    Cached mode
> > +    iocachectr == 0   Idle. No files open, no passthrough
>
> More accurately, no open files in caching or passthough.
> files open with FOPEN_DIRECT_IO may exist and they
> do not deny entering either caching or passthrough iomode.
>
> > +    iocachectr < 0    Uncached/passthrough mode
> > +
> > +Each open file in passthrough mode holds one reference (``iocachectr--``).
> > +The inode-level passthrough setup holds one additional long-lived reference
> > +if the backing has inode ops (getattr/setattr). This long-lived reference is
> > +released on inode eviction.
> > +
> > +Cached mode and passthrough mode are mutually exclusive. Attempting either
> > +while the other is active returns ``-ETXTBSY``.
> > +
> > +For directories, the same mechanism arbitrates between cached readdir
> > +(``FOPEN_CACHE_DIR``) and passthrough readdir. A directory opened without
> > +``FOPEN_CACHE_DIR`` and without ``FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH`` is treated as direct I/O
> > +and does not affect io mode.
> > +
> > +Things to note
> > +--------------
> > +
> > +- ``FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO`` requires 64-bit ``ino_t``.
>
> Not relevant I think.
>
> > +- Readdirplus does not set up inode passthrough. Inodes created via readdirplus
> > +  use normal FUSE operations until a fresh lookup occurs.
> > +- An inode's backing association is set once and cannot be changed.
> > +- Passthrough and cached I/O cannot coexist on the same inode.
> > +- If any inode operations are passed through, this means all opened files need
> > +  to set the ``FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH`` flag in the open response, even if reads
> > +  and writes are not passed through. If reads and writes are not passed
> > +  through, they will go directly to the daemon.
>
> I had not considered this.
> Setting OP_GETATTR/SETATTR in LOOKUP without any OP_RW bits
> and then requiring that the server open files with FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH
> with the backing_id sounds strange -
> I understand why it was done this way, but for users this could be perplexing.
>
> The thing is that if the server is only doing GETATTR passthrough there is not
> really a reason to deny open file in caching mode (I think?).
>
> The limitation that requires FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH is encoded in
> fuse_file_cached_io_open()
>     fuse_is_io_cache_wait()
>         !fuse_inode_backing()
>
> We have two options.
>
> Either we decide that server passing through GETATTR and opening
> files in caching mode is not interesting to support (for now) and then just
> maybe we need to better document the reason that server needs to
> use FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH despite not doing io passthrough.
>
> Or we relax the limitation w.r.t cached io mode, something like this
> (completely untested most likely broken based or earlier review
> suggestion regarding fuse_passthrough_op):
>
> Thanks,
> Amir.
>
> diff --git a/fs/fuse/iomode.c b/fs/fuse/iomode.c
> index c5f3e2201c6ab..7c33e7a974f6c 100644
> --- a/fs/fuse/iomode.c
> +++ b/fs/fuse/iomode.c
> @@ -20,7 +20,8 @@ static inline bool fuse_is_io_cache_wait(struct inode *inode)
>         struct fuse_inode *fi = get_fuse_inode(inode);
>
>         return S_ISREG(inode->i_mode) &&
> -               READ_ONCE(fi->iocachectr) < 0 && !fuse_inode_backing(fi);
> +               READ_ONCE(fi->iocachectr) < 0 &&
> +               !fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS);
>  }

Obviously, this was naive and not close to enough, because
fuse_inode_set_passthrough() still takes the negative iocachectr
refcount.

Please ignore this. Supporting caching iomode and GETATTR
passthrough is left for a future exercise, if anyone is ever interested.

Thanks,
Amir.

---

On Sat, May 16, 2026 at 2:52 AM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> With FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO, the backing file is associated with the inode
> at lookup time. Allow the server to pass in backing_id=0 in the open
> response. On the kernel side, this uses the backing file already set up
> on the inode.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>

Reviewed-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>

> ---

> index d62a1c751157..ae0137caa06d 100644
> --- a/fs/fuse/passthrough.c
> +++ b/fs/fuse/passthrough.c

>   */
>  struct fuse_backing *fuse_passthrough_open(struct file *file, int backing_id)

> +       struct fuse_inode *fi = get_fuse_inode(file_inode(file));
>         struct fuse_file *ff = file->private_data;
>         struct fuse_conn *fc = ff->fm->fc;

>         struct file *backing_file;

>

>         if (IS_ERR_OR_NULL(fb)) {

>

On Sat, May 16, 2026 at 2:52 AM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Use the new/extended struct fuse_entry2_out for lookups. If a backing id

The 3 lookup patches look fine in general, but I will review them more
closely after I have a clear understanding on the desired protocol.

Thanks,
Amir.

> +static int fuse_process_entry2(struct fuse_conn *fc,

review_comments.summary: Amir Goldstein noted that d_backing_inode() is a noop and has 177 call sites, requesting that these be converted to d_inode() and the function removed.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, May 16, 2026 at 2:53 AM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Move logic in ovl_copyattr() to a generic backing_inode_copyattr()
> function in a new fs/backing-inode.c, which other filesystems that use
> backing inodes (eg fuse passthrough) will use.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>

>  fs/overlayfs/util.c           | 33 +++------------------------
>  include/linux/backing-inode.h | 14 ++++++++++++


Nice!

Please add these files to
FILESYSTEMS [STACKABLE]
section in MAINTAINERS.

Otherwise, feel free to add:
Reviewed-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>

There is one more cleanup patch required, now or later, to get rid of
some awkwardness in kernel fs code.
If you don't want to deal with it, I can take this cleanup later.

d_backing_inode() is a noop from the day it was added and has 177
call sites. Those call sites should be converted to d_inode() and
d_backing_inode() should go away.

backing_inode_security{,_novalidate}() are likewise unneeded
dups of inode_security{,_novalidate}() and should be removed.

Thanks,
Amir.

> >
> > Add passthrough setattr which sets attributes directly on the backing
> > inode through backing_inode_setattr() instead of sending FUSE_SETATTR to
> > the server.
> >
> > Passthrough setattr is checked before the
> > handle_killpriv/handle_killpriv_v2 suid/sgid stripping because the
> > stripping is handled natively by notify_change() on the backing inode.
> >
> > Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
> > ---
> >  fs/fuse/dir.c             |  3 +++
> >  fs/fuse/fuse_i.h          |  3 ++-

> >  4 files changed, 34 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
